Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## Paces

### release-ceremony-and-bcg-command-lint (₢BBABM) [rough]

## Character

Was mechanical ceremony repair; now carries a net-new Rust static-analysis fixture with a real design surface. Three mechanical tracks (ceremony retirement cleanup, claimed-done fixes, verification sweep) frame one design-laden track: a BCG command-compliance linter in theurge. The linter needs judgment; the rest is mechanical. The locked decisions below are settled — do not relitigate them; mount-time minting of the new module/fixture name is the open naming call.

## Locked decisions (2026-05-31 design conversation, Opus 4.8)

- **Track 2 is a Rust theurge lint, not bash.** The external-command audit becomes a static-analysis fixture in theurge's **fast test suite** — not a bash step in `rbq_qualify_release()`. It rides `rbw-ts.fast` (dev loop) and, via the `complete` suite, `rbw-tr` (release gate), which the ceremony's Step 4 runs pre-strip. That coverage is what lets ceremony **Step 2 be deleted**.
- **BCG is the single source of truth.** Encode the allowlists from BCG section "Command Dependency Discipline": the POSIX floor, the declared-dependency set (RBS0 inventory + `openssl`), and the **eviction table — enforced**, failing with BCG's prescribed replacement (e.g. `grep` -> `case`/`[[ =~ ]]`). This is stricter than today's Step 2, whose hand-rolled POSIX list wrongly permits evicted commands.
- **Algorithm: two-pass, function-aware.** Collect every locally-defined function across the corpus; then flag each command-position token not in {bash builtins, local functions, POSIX allowlist, declared deps}, and fail-with-replacement on the eviction table. Soundness rests on presumed BCG-compliance (shellcheck already gates the corpus in `rbq_qualify_fast`) — a command-position scan suffices; no heredoc/case parser.
- **Checks everything pre-veiling.** Lints ALL `Tools/` bash including `vov_veiled/`. The linter carries **no veil-scope knowledge** — it is a linter, not veiling logic, and must not couple to either packaging path (`vof` kit-parcel collection, or the ceremony strip). The only partition is by **execution environment**: kit-bash (strict BCG) vs GCB-bash (`rbgja/`, `rbgjb/`, `rbgjv/` — looser; gcloud et al. present). **Encode the GCB-domain allowlist now**; do not skip those dirs.
- **First-run cleanup is in-pace.** Checking veiled bash for the first time may surface real eviction-table violations; fix them in this pace, do not split to a follow-on. Expect the first run not to land green.
- **Track 4 resolved.** `OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM` pointing at `recipebottle-staging.git` is **intended**. No remote mutation. Ensure no ceremony/doc text asserts the non-staging URL as canonical.

## Tracks

### Track 1 — Ceremony retirement cleanup

In `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md`: Step 8 — drop the retired `cp README.consumer.md README.md` (keep the `CLAUDE.consumer.md` copy); the lowercase `readme.md` removal is a no-op (case-insensitive alias of the tracked `README.md`) — replace it with defensive `git rm -f --ignore-unmatch index.html .nojekyll`; Step 10 survival list — remove `.nojekyll` and `index.html` (both retired); staging — keep `git add CLAUDE.md` (Step 8 overwrites it), drop the explicit `README.md` add (`git add -u` covers it). Step 2's dependency-source line vanishes with Step 2 (Track 2).

### Track 2 — BCG command-lint fixture (locked above)

Build per the locked decisions. Model on the host-side fast-fixture pattern (`rbtdrf_fast.rs`: `dir: &Path -> rbtdre_Verdict`, registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`, added to the `fast` suite). Mint a fresh anchor word — `audit` is taken (image-domain cult verb). Then delete ceremony Step 2.

### Track 3 — Verify claimed-done

Item B is a FIX: correct the phantom `tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` -> `tt/rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh` in ceremony Step 11 (`rbw-tf` is FixtureRun). Items C (Step 9 marshal-zero ordering before the Step 10 strip) and D (Step 3 regime-completeness check present) verify-and-close.

### Track 5 — Verification sweep

After Tracks 1-4 land: retirement grep (no live `index.html`/`README.consumer.md`/`.nojekyll` outside retired gallops/memos); foundry colophon grep (no stale `rbw-h*` foundry refs in consumer-visible sources post b63dc6cf); fast qualify `tt/rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh`; onboarding smoke (`tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh` + one role track); notch. No auto-wrap — operator decides the close.

## Verification gates

Each track presented before executing. Review point before committing the ceremony diff. The lint fixture's first full run (including veiled bash) is a discovery step, not a pass gate — surface violations before fixing. No remote mutation. No auto-wrap.

## References

- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — ceremony repair target
- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md` section "Command Dependency Discipline" — allowlist source of truth (POSIX floor, eviction table, declared-dep principle)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs`, `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fast-fixture shape + suite/manifest registration
- `Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh` — `rbq_qualify_fast`/`rbq_qualify_release`; shellcheck sibling; Step 2 deletion rationale
- `Tools/vok/vof/` — existing kit-parcel veiling (`vofr_collect` excludes vov_veiled); the linter must NOT couple to it
- Commits b7c1dbdd (retirement), 5dc8dcc6 (README restructure), b63dc6cf (foundry colophon rbw-h* -> rbw-f*)
- Absorbed ₢A8AAA D4+D5; D3 (CLAUDE.consumer glossary) landed on 5dc8dcc6, excluded

### prep-release-integration (₢BBAAH) [rough]

## Character

Final integration pace. Wires pristine-pass as a precondition for `/rbk-prep-release` so the upstream contribution ceremony cannot proceed without a recent pristine green. Closes the loop: pristine qualification becomes load-bearing on release prep, not optional ceremony.

## Work

Modify `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` to add a precondition step: verify rbw-tP ran green against the current HEAD. If not, prep-release fails-fast with operator-visible message naming the marshal-zero → pristine sequence.

Recency contract is **exact-HEAD-match** — strongest invariant, prevents stale passes. rbw-tP green tally writes an artifact linking pass to HEAD's commit SHA; `/rbk-prep-release` reads it and refuses on mismatch. Artifact is gitignored (deliberately not committed). Artifact mechanism (file location, contents) is a mount-time decision.

Update README "Release Procedure" section to reflect that the prep-release step now enforces rbw-tP had to pass against the very HEAD that prep-release is preparing. README references the post-success cleanup ceremony documented in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI).

- After successful rbw-tP, `/rbk-prep-release` proceeds normally
- After successful rbw-tP followed by an unrelated commit, `/rbk-prep-release` refuses (SHA mismatch)
- Pristine-passed artifact is gitignored
- README precondition documentation references the cleanup ceremony in RELEASE.md

## Out of scope

- Cleanup ceremony documentation in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI)
- Restructuring the rest of `/rbk-prep-release` (preserved as-is)
- Multi-pristine-run aggregation

### rbs0-sync-sweep-from-bb-changes (₢BBAAq) [rough]

## Character

## Known sync points (running list — append as paces land)

- **`RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe.adoc`** (from BBAAc inline fix on the JWT-SA probe — IAM-policy-read propagation race absorption). The procedural spec describes the OLD semantic: "all iterations must pass; HTTP 403 → fatal access denied; HTTP 401 → fatal token rejected." New code semantic in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`: bounded propagation-absorbing retry — first 2xx wins, HTTP 403 retries within `max_attempts × delay_ms` budget, all other codes (including 401) fall to a single fail-fast catch-all with body-summary diagnostic via `zrbgv_jq_error_message_capture`. Sections needing rewrite: §"Execute Probe Iterations" (rename concept to *attempts*; rewrite "Evaluate Response" sub-section to reflect 2xx-wins / 403-retry / else-fatal); §"Display Summary" ("succeeded after «I» attempt(s)" instead of "«N»/«N» iterations passed"); the top NOTE block (intent shift from sustained-access verification to propagation-window absorption + verification); §"Validate Input Parameters" (`ITERATION_COUNT` description shifts to "max attempts — first 2xx wins"; `DELAY_MS` to "between retries"). Companion call-site change: `rbte_engine.sh::rbte_probe` defaults shifted to 60 × 3000ms (≈180s budget) for SA roles only; Payor probe defaults and semantic unchanged.

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢BBAAx) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAT in ₣A-.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### fast-qualify-shellcheck-fold-cover-zero (₢BBABG) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical. Fold the two fast-codebase-check entry points into one tier, parallelize the slow one, expand its coverage, bring its baseline to zero, and wire it into marshal-zero as an early structural gate.

## Locked decisions

- **One fast-tier shellcheck entry.** `rbq_qualify_fast` absorbs `buq_shellcheck`; standalone `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` retires. The primitive function stays; only the standalone tabtarget retires. `rbw-tr` drops its now-redundant shellcheck phase.
- **Parallelize via xargs -P.** Replace the single shellcheck invocation with xargs-driven parallel invocation; aggregate exit code into one verdict. Target ~1–2s wall-clock for the file set (down from ~9s).
- **Coverage expansion.** `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh` joins the shellcheck file set. Mount-time call on scope-generalization shape (multi-root parameter vs explicit outside-Tools list).
- **Baseline zero.** Every current shellcheck finding either fixed in-place or moved to `busc_shellcheckrc` with BCG-structural rationale. Fast-tier exits zero on clean tree after wrap.
- **MZ early gate.** `rblm_zero` runs the parallelized shellcheck primitive in its pre-check block (alongside tree-clean / upstream / pushed). Non-zero shellcheck fails marshal-zero before it touches anything. Gauntlet entry-state cannot accept a shellcheck-dirty codebase by construction.

## Wrap criterion

`tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` exits zero, runs in ~2s wall-clock, covers `Tools/` plus `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh`. `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` no longer exists. `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` no longer makes a separate shellcheck call. `tt/rbw-MZ.MarshalZeroes.sh` fails fast on any shellcheck finding before mutating anything.

## Out of scope

- `tt/*.sh` shellcheck coverage (`buq_tabtargets` shape-check is the appropriate gate for generated shims)
- `.buk/launcher.*.sh` (3–4 line trivial shims; capture as itch if ever produces a finding)
- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/` subtree — operator call at mount (exempt vs fix)

### foundry-account-state-invalid-tolerance (₢BBABL) [rough]

## Character
Mechanical retry-tolerance extension; shape locked by the memo. Two touchpoints, one error class. Surfaced by dogfight verification (2 failures / 3 runs — transient).

## Source
`Memos/memo-20260527-account-state-invalid-reinvest-flap.md` — full 401/`ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` signature, two-host evidence, the prior-art gap, and the time-bound tolerance model to reuse.

## Shape (locked)
- `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` build-status poll loop: absorb a transient `401 UNAUTHENTICATED` + `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` with bounded backoff instead of counting it toward the consecutive-error fatal — a still-`WORKING` build must survive a director-SA auth flap.
- `rbgo_OAuth.sh` consumer-retry token mint: extend the existing retry error-class to `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID`, covering the `rbfv` vouch path (Host B in the memo).
- The retry budget is the discriminator (settling completes within budget; real denial waits it out and fails clean) — reuse the established propagation / access-probe time-bound model rather than minting new literals.

## Done
A director re-invest immediately followed by `ordain` survives the flap: the transient is logged and retried within budget, never fatal on a healthy build. Confirm the `rbfv` vouch failure body is the same class (memo caveat).

### foundry-and-cloud-step-trust-audit (₢BBABN) [rough]

Drafted from ₢AUAA5 in ₣AU.

## Character

Research-heavy with judgment — produces a ranked findings document; no code changes in this pace. Parallels the prior `oauth-trust-hygiene-sweep` review method (₢A_AAN) but widened to the foundry surface, including the step scripts deployed INTO Cloud Build workers (a distinct trust boundary from the on-curia orchestrating bash). Remediation is explicitly deferred: each finding cluster that the operator elects to pursue gets its own follow-up pace, slated by the operator after reviewing the findings.

## Scope

### On-curia orchestrating bash (rbf* family)

| File | Role |
|------|------|
| `Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` | Shared foundry primitives (~52 KB) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` | Director build orchestration — conjure/bind/graft/enshrine/inscribe dispatch (~72 KB, largest file in the family) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` | Hallmark ledger — abjure/tally/jettison/rekons/wrest (~36 KB) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfr_FoundryRetriever.sh` | Retriever operations — summon, plumb (~12 KB) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh` | Verify — vouch emit and consume (~39 KB) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfc_cli.sh`, `rbfd_cli.sh`, `rbfl_cli.sh`, `rbfr_cli.sh`, `rbfv_cli.sh` | CLI dispatchers |

### Cloud Build step scripts (deployed into Cloud Build workers — distinct trust boundary)

These scripts run INSIDE Cloud Build workers with Cloud Build's identity, not the operator's identity. They receive parameters via `_RBG*` substitutions that the director renders into jq-composed build step bodies. They are a distinct trust surface from the orchestrating bash: quoting hazards land differently (step scripts run in a minimal container); error handling in-builder affects what exit codes the builder surfaces; step scripts see only what the director sends.

| Directory | Count | Role |
|-----------|------:|------|
| `Tools/rbk/rbgja/` | 4 | About assembly — discover-platforms.py, syft-per-platform.sh, build-info-per-platform.py, assemble-push-about.sh |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjb/` | 6 | Buildx — derive-tag-base, qemu-binfmt, buildx-push-image, per-platform-pullback, push-per-platform, push-diags |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgje/` | 1 | Enshrine — copy |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgji/` | 1 | Inscribe — mirror |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjm/` | 1 | Mirror-image |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjv/` | 3 | Vouch/verify — download-verifier, verify-provenance.py, assemble-push-vouch |

Three of these are Python (`rbgja01`, `rbgja03`, `rbgjv02`) — audited with the same trust-foment lens adapted to Python idioms.

## Method

Apply the same tiered trust-foment framework used in the OAuth review (₢A_AAN), adapted to foundry concerns:

### Tier 1 — visible-in-30-seconds signals

- Shellcheck cleanliness across all foundry bash and step-script bash
- `set -euo pipefail` presence and placement
- Quoting and expansion discipline (especially for parameters that flow into jq, curl, skopeo, docker buildx, oras, etc.)
- Python: lint (ruff/pyflakes if available), `shell=True` subprocess use, unvalidated input
- TODO/FIXME/XXX density, especially undated or in supply-chain-adjacent paths
- Dead code, commented-out blocks, unused constants

### Tier 2 — second-look signals

- **Error handling asymmetry across the rbf* family** — do rbfc, rbfd, rbfl, rbfr, rbfv fail the same way for the same class of problem?
- **Duplication across step scripts** — rbgjb03/rbgjb05 buildx paths, rbgja04/rbgjv03 assemble-push variants
- **Stale references after recent structural work** — with `₢A_AAB` RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX threading and `₢A_AAK` categorical layout changes landing in A_, check for drift, half-migrated paths, or comments that contradict current code
- **TOCTOU / race-adjacent patterns** — temp file creation, permission tightening order
- **Error-swallowing patterns** — `|| true` placements, subshell failure masking, pipefail gaps

### Tier 3 — foundry-specific trust surfaces

1. **Supply-chain chain of custody** — ordain → vouch → summon → plumb. What does each ark promise, and does the consumer actually verify what was promised? Specifically:
   - SLSA provenance: `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` is the verifier. Does the vouch artifact it checks against actually carry what the director claimed to emit?
   - Vouch gate: can a conjure/bind/graft succeed without a matching vouch? Does `rbfr` refuse to summon an un-vouched hallmark?
   - Cross-mode (conjure vs. bind vs. graft): each has different provenance guarantees. Does the mode-aware vouch logic in `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh` correctly distinguish, or are there paths where a graft verdict can leak into a conjure-mode verification?

2. **Multi-platform build integrity** — buildx per-platform pullback, digest handling, and platform-specific SBOM/about emission. Does the per-platform pipeline cross-contaminate? Can a push-per-platform step silently push an image built for the wrong platform?

3. **Cloud Build step-script input validation** — step scripts receive `_RBG*` substitutions composed by the director via jq `--arg` and `--argjson`. jq quoting is correct by construction, but:
   - Any place the director uses raw string interpolation into a build YAML/JSON?
   - Any step script that interpolates its own environment into a shell command without quoting?
   - Any step script that accepts an argv parameter without validating its shape?

4. **Cloud Build posture verification** — the step container environment. What's in it? What can it reach? RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc is the stated posture; does step-script behavior match, or does any step script make assumptions incompatible with the stated posture (e.g., expecting network reachability that airgap pool denies)?

5. **Secret handling in step scripts** — step scripts should see only what they need. Check for:
   - Secret Manager refs correctly scoped per step
   - No secrets logged to Cloud Build logs (Google mirrors these; log leaks are persistent)
   - No secrets written to intermediate build artifacts (e.g., baked into image layers)
   - No secrets embedded in step-script source itself

6. **Container-registry write authority** — which step pushes to which path? Can a graft step push into a hallmark path meant for conjure? (The AAK categorical layout tightens this, but worth auditing once it lands.)

7. **Verifier/verified symmetry** — the vouch schema and the provenance-verify schema must stay in lockstep. If `rbgjv03-assemble-push-vouch.sh` emits field X and `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` checks field Y, divergence is a silent trust gap.

8. **Dependency pins inside step scripts** — step scripts pull tools (syft, skopeo, oras, cosign if present, docker buildx, qemu-binfmt, gcloud-within-builder). Are these pinned by digest? By tag? Not at all? The RBSRG (RegimeGcbPins) spec is the reference contract; does reality match?

9. **Failure modes visible to operator** — when a cloud build step fails, what does the operator see? Cryptic exit codes, clean structured errors, or a wall of unattributed output? This is a trust signal for the operator experience more than for an external auditor, but both matter.

10. **Python ↔ bash boundary** — `rbgja01-discover-platforms.py`, `rbgja03-build-info-per-platform.py`, `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py`. These read bash-set environment, emit JSON to stdout or files, and signal failure via exit code. The boundary is where most cross-language bugs live. Check: JSON emission discipline (one doc per invocation, no stray stdout), exit-code fidelity, schema stability.

## Deliverable

A findings report written to the chat (not committed to disk unless operator requests) matching the format produced for ₢A_AAN:

- **"What builds trust here"** section — specific patterns worth preserving (setting the floor)
- **Tier 1 / 2 / 3 findings** — each numbered (F1, F2, …), each naming file:line, describing the trust-foment signal, and proposing a specific fix or clarification
- **Priority list** — findings ranked by trust-yield-per-line-of-diff so the operator can pick which to pursue first

The operator then reviews and elects which clusters to remediate. Each elected cluster becomes its own follow-up pace, either in AU (if pre-release scope) or in A_ (if structural enough to block depot regen). F-numbering is internal to this audit and does not carry across pace boundaries.

## Relation to other work

- **₢A_AAN** (oauth-trust-hygiene-sweep) already covers the OAuth surface (`rbgo_OAuth.sh`, `rbgp_Payor.sh` OAuth portion, `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`, `rbro_regime.sh`, `rbro_cli.sh`). This foundry audit does NOT revisit those files.
- **F7 follow-up** (factor three token-exchange code paths, still to be slated in A_) is also OAuth-scoped and not revisited here.
- **AAK / AAL / AAB** (GAR categorical layout and prefix threading in A_) are actively reshaping paths the foundry constructs. The audit acknowledges this: findings about path construction should be cross-referenced against AAK's helper-module intent (`rbgl_GarLayout.sh`) to avoid flagging things AAK already plans to fix. Ideally this audit runs AFTER AAK lands, so the audit reviews the post-migration state rather than transitional code.
- **Recent AU paces** touched `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` (lanes S/A/J/K/F — build provenance, multiplatform, vouch preflight, digest extraction) and `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` (lane F). The audit reads the current state; it does not revisit decisions those paces made, only flags if the implementations embody trust-foment signals visible at the code surface.

## Out of scope

- Implementing any fixes (separate per-cluster paces — operator's decision)
- Auditing OAuth code (covered by ₢A_AAN and its forthcoming F7 sibling)
- Auditing non-foundry / non-step-script RBK code (governor, IAM, access-probe — separate audits if wanted)
- Auditing JJK, BUK, CMK, VVK — all out-of-kit scope
- Pen-testing the Cloud Build environment itself (that's an external-service posture question — see RBSCB; audit only compares script behavior to stated posture)
- Changing the stated Cloud Build posture (RBSCB is a design decision; audit observes, doesn't propose)
- Regenerating pinned digest references (RBSRG is a contract; audit checks adherence, doesn't rotate)

## Verification (for the audit pace itself)

This is a research pace; the "verification" is that the deliverable exists, names real file:line references, is internally consistent, and the operator reads it and either accepts it as-is or asks for clarifications before deciding on follow-ups.

- Every finding names `file:line` (verifiable by inspection)
- Every finding proposes a specific remediation OR explicitly marks "observation-only"
- Findings are ranked (not a flat list)
- Priority table clusters related findings so follow-up paces can be scoped cleanly
- "What builds trust here" section present — audits that only find faults foment their own kind of distrust

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)

### release-ceremony-and-bcg-command-lint (₢BBABM) [rough]

## Character

Was mechanical ceremony repair; now carries a net-new Rust static-analysis fixture with a real design surface. Three mechanical tracks (ceremony retirement cleanup, claimed-done fixes, verification sweep) frame one design-laden track: a BCG command-compliance linter in theurge. The linter needs judgment; the rest is mechanical. The locked decisions below are settled — do not relitigate them; mount-time minting of the new module/fixture name is the open naming call.

## Locked decisions (2026-05-31 design conversation, Opus 4.8)

- **Track 2 is a Rust theurge lint, not bash.** The external-command audit becomes a static-analysis fixture in theurge's **fast test suite** — not a bash step in `rbq_qualify_release()`. It rides `rbw-ts.fast` (dev loop) and, via the `complete` suite, `rbw-tr` (release gate), which the ceremony's Step 4 runs pre-strip. That coverage is what lets ceremony **Step 2 be deleted**.
- **BCG is the single source of truth.** Encode the allowlists from BCG section "Command Dependency Discipline": the POSIX floor, the declared-dependency set (RBS0 inventory + `openssl`), and the **eviction table — enforced**, failing with BCG's prescribed replacement (e.g. `grep` -> `case`/`[[ =~ ]]`). This is stricter than today's Step 2, whose hand-rolled POSIX list wrongly permits evicted commands.
- **Algorithm: two-pass, function-aware.** Collect every locally-defined function across the corpus; then flag each command-position token not in {bash builtins, local functions, POSIX allowlist, declared deps}, and fail-with-replacement on the eviction table. Soundness rests on presumed BCG-compliance (shellcheck already gates the corpus in `rbq_qualify_fast`) — a command-position scan suffices; no heredoc/case parser.
- **Checks everything pre-veiling.** Lints ALL `Tools/` bash including `vov_veiled/`. The linter carries **no veil-scope knowledge** — it is a linter, not veiling logic, and must not couple to either packaging path (`vof` kit-parcel collection, or the ceremony strip). The only partition is by **execution environment**: kit-bash (strict BCG) vs GCB-bash (`rbgja/`, `rbgjb/`, `rbgjv/` — looser; gcloud et al. present). **Encode the GCB-domain allowlist now**; do not skip those dirs.
- **First-run cleanup is in-pace.** Checking veiled bash for the first time may surface real eviction-table violations; fix them in this pace, do not split to a follow-on. Expect the first run not to land green.
- **Track 4 resolved.** `OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM` pointing at `recipebottle-staging.git` is **intended**. No remote mutation. Ensure no ceremony/doc text asserts the non-staging URL as canonical.

## Tracks

### Track 1 — Ceremony retirement cleanup

In `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md`: Step 8 — drop the retired `cp README.consumer.md README.md` (keep the `CLAUDE.consumer.md` copy); the lowercase `readme.md` removal is a no-op (case-insensitive alias of the tracked `README.md`) — replace it with defensive `git rm -f --ignore-unmatch index.html .nojekyll`; Step 10 survival list — remove `.nojekyll` and `index.html` (both retired); staging — keep `git add CLAUDE.md` (Step 8 overwrites it), drop the explicit `README.md` add (`git add -u` covers it). Step 2's dependency-source line vanishes with Step 2 (Track 2).

### Track 2 — BCG command-lint fixture (locked above)

Build per the locked decisions. Model on the host-side fast-fixture pattern (`rbtdrf_fast.rs`: `dir: &Path -> rbtdre_Verdict`, registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`, added to the `fast` suite). Mint a fresh anchor word — `audit` is taken (image-domain cult verb). Then delete ceremony Step 2.

### Track 3 — Verify claimed-done

Item B is a FIX: correct the phantom `tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` -> `tt/rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh` in ceremony Step 11 (`rbw-tf` is FixtureRun). Items C (Step 9 marshal-zero ordering before the Step 10 strip) and D (Step 3 regime-completeness check present) verify-and-close.

### Track 5 — Verification sweep

After Tracks 1-4 land: retirement grep (no live `index.html`/`README.consumer.md`/`.nojekyll` outside retired gallops/memos); foundry colophon grep (no stale `rbw-h*` foundry refs in consumer-visible sources post b63dc6cf); fast qualify `tt/rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh`; onboarding smoke (`tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh` + one role track); notch. No auto-wrap — operator decides the close.

## Verification gates

Each track presented before executing. Review point before committing the ceremony diff. The lint fixture's first full run (including veiled bash) is a discovery step, not a pass gate — surface violations before fixing. No remote mutation. No auto-wrap.

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢BBAAx) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAT in ₣A-.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### Cloud Build step scripts (deployed into Cloud Build workers — distinct trust boundary)

These scripts run INSIDE Cloud Build workers with Cloud Build's identity, not the operator's identity. They receive parameters via `_RBG*` substitutions that the director renders into jq-composed build step bodies. They are a distinct trust surface from the orchestrating bash: quoting hazards land differently (step scripts run in a minimal container); error handling in-builder affects what exit codes the builder surfaces; step scripts see only what the director sends.

| Directory | Count | Role |
|-----------|------:|------|
| `Tools/rbk/rbgja/` | 4 | About assembly — discover-platforms.py, syft-per-platform.sh, build-info-per-platform.py, assemble-push-about.sh |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjb/` | 6 | Buildx — derive-tag-base, qemu-binfmt, buildx-push-image, per-platform-pullback, push-per-platform, push-diags |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgje/` | 1 | Enshrine — copy |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgji/` | 1 | Inscribe — mirror |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjm/` | 1 | Mirror-image |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjv/` | 3 | Vouch/verify — download-verifier, verify-provenance.py, assemble-push-vouch |

Three of these are Python (`rbgja01`, `rbgja03`, `rbgjv02`) — audited with the same trust-foment lens adapted to Python idioms.

## Method

Apply the same tiered trust-foment framework used in the OAuth review (₢A_AAN), adapted to foundry concerns:

### Tier 1 — visible-in-30-seconds signals

- Shellcheck cleanliness across all foundry bash and step-script bash
- `set -euo pipefail` presence and placement
- Quoting and expansion discipline (especially for parameters that flow into jq, curl, skopeo, docker buildx, oras, etc.)
- Python: lint (ruff/pyflakes if available), `shell=True` subprocess use, unvalidated input
- TODO/FIXME/XXX density, especially undated or in supply-chain-adjacent paths
- Dead code, commented-out blocks, unused constants

### Tier 2 — second-look signals

- **Error handling asymmetry across the rbf* family** — do rbfc, rbfd, rbfl, rbfr, rbfv fail the same way for the same class of problem?
- **Duplication across step scripts** — rbgjb03/rbgjb05 buildx paths, rbgja04/rbgjv03 assemble-push variants
- **Stale references after recent structural work** — with `₢A_AAB` RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX threading and `₢A_AAK` categorical layout changes landing in A_, check for drift, half-migrated paths, or comments that contradict current code
- **TOCTOU / race-adjacent patterns** — temp file creation, permission tightening order
- **Error-swallowing patterns** — `|| true` placements, subshell failure masking, pipefail gaps

### Tier 3 — foundry-specific trust surfaces

1. **Supply-chain chain of custody** — ordain → vouch → summon → plumb. What does each ark promise, and does the consumer actually verify what was promised? Specifically:
   - SLSA provenance: `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` is the verifier. Does the vouch artifact it checks against actually carry what the director claimed to emit?
   - Vouch gate: can a conjure/bind/graft succeed without a matching vouch? Does `rbfr` refuse to summon an un-vouched hallmark?
   - Cross-mode (conjure vs. bind vs. graft): each has different provenance guarantees. Does the mode-aware vouch logic in `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh` correctly distinguish, or are there paths where a graft verdict can leak into a conjure-mode verification?

2. **Multi-platform build integrity** — buildx per-platform pullback, digest handling, and platform-specific SBOM/about emission. Does the per-platform pipeline cross-contaminate? Can a push-per-platform step silently push an image built for the wrong platform?

3. **Cloud Build step-script input validation** — step scripts receive `_RBG*` substitutions composed by the director via jq `--arg` and `--argjson`. jq quoting is correct by construction, but:
   - Any place the director uses raw string interpolation into a build YAML/JSON?
   - Any step script that interpolates its own environment into a shell command without quoting?
   - Any step script that accepts an argv parameter without validating its shape?

4. **Cloud Build posture verification** — the step container environment. What's in it? What can it reach? RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc is the stated posture; does step-script behavior match, or does any step script make assumptions incompatible with the stated posture (e.g., expecting network reachability that airgap pool denies)?

5. **Secret handling in step scripts** — step scripts should see only what they need. Check for:
   - Secret Manager refs correctly scoped per step
   - No secrets logged to Cloud Build logs (Google mirrors these; log leaks are persistent)
   - No secrets written to intermediate build artifacts (e.g., baked into image layers)
   - No secrets embedded in step-script source itself

6. **Container-registry write authority** — which step pushes to which path? Can a graft step push into a hallmark path meant for conjure? (The AAK categorical layout tightens this, but worth auditing once it lands.)

7. **Verifier/verified symmetry** — the vouch schema and the provenance-verify schema must stay in lockstep. If `rbgjv03-assemble-push-vouch.sh` emits field X and `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` checks field Y, divergence is a silent trust gap.

8. **Dependency pins inside step scripts** — step scripts pull tools (syft, skopeo, oras, cosign if present, docker buildx, qemu-binfmt, gcloud-within-builder). Are these pinned by digest? By tag? Not at all? The RBSRG (RegimeGcbPins) spec is the reference contract; does reality match?

9. **Failure modes visible to operator** — when a cloud build step fails, what does the operator see? Cryptic exit codes, clean structured errors, or a wall of unattributed output? This is a trust signal for the operator experience more than for an external auditor, but both matter.

10. **Python ↔ bash boundary** — `rbgja01-discover-platforms.py`, `rbgja03-build-info-per-platform.py`, `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py`. These read bash-set environment, emit JSON to stdout or files, and signal failure via exit code. The boundary is where most cross-language bugs live. Check: JSON emission discipline (one doc per invocation, no stray stdout), exit-code fidelity, schema stability.

## Deliverable

A findings report written to the chat (not committed to disk unless operator requests) matching the format produced for ₢A_AAN:

- **"What builds trust here"** section — specific patterns worth preserving (setting the floor)
- **Tier 1 / 2 / 3 findings** — each numbered (F1, F2, …), each naming file:line, describing the trust-foment signal, and proposing a specific fix or clarification
- **Priority list** — findings ranked by trust-yield-per-line-of-diff so the operator can pick which to pursue first

The operator then reviews and elects which clusters to remediate. Each elected cluster becomes its own follow-up pace, either in AU (if pre-release scope) or in A_ (if structural enough to block depot regen). F-numbering is internal to this audit and does not carry across pace boundaries.

## Relation to other work

- **₢A_AAN** (oauth-trust-hygiene-sweep) already covers the OAuth surface (`rbgo_OAuth.sh`, `rbgp_Payor.sh` OAuth portion, `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`, `rbro_regime.sh`, `rbro_cli.sh`). This foundry audit does NOT revisit those files.
- **F7 follow-up** (factor three token-exchange code paths, still to be slated in A_) is also OAuth-scoped and not revisited here.
- **AAK / AAL / AAB** (GAR categorical layout and prefix threading in A_) are actively reshaping paths the foundry constructs. The audit acknowledges this: findings about path construction should be cross-referenced against AAK's helper-module intent (`rbgl_GarLayout.sh`) to avoid flagging things AAK already plans to fix. Ideally this audit runs AFTER AAK lands, so the audit reviews the post-migration state rather than transitional code.
- **Recent AU paces** touched `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` (lanes S/A/J/K/F — build provenance, multiplatform, vouch preflight, digest extraction) and `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` (lane F). The audit reads the current state; it does not revisit decisions those paces made, only flags if the implementations embody trust-foment signals visible at the code surface.

## Out of scope

- Implementing any fixes (separate per-cluster paces — operator's decision)
- Auditing OAuth code (covered by ₢A_AAN and its forthcoming F7 sibling)
- Auditing non-foundry / non-step-script RBK code (governor, IAM, access-probe — separate audits if wanted)
- Auditing JJK, BUK, CMK, VVK — all out-of-kit scope
- Pen-testing the Cloud Build environment itself (that's an external-service posture question — see RBSCB; audit only compares script behavior to stated posture)
- Changing the stated Cloud Build posture (RBSCB is a design decision; audit observes, doesn't propose)
- Regenerating pinned digest references (RBSRG is a contract; audit checks adherence, doesn't rotate)

## Verification (for the audit pace itself)

This is a research pace; the "verification" is that the deliverable exists, names real file:line references, is internally consistent, and the operator reads it and either accepts it as-is or asks for clarifications before deciding on follow-ups.

- Every finding names `file:line` (verifiable by inspection)
- Every finding proposes a specific remediation OR explicitly marks "observation-only"
- Findings are ranked (not a flat list)
- Priority table clusters related findings so follow-up paces can be scoped cleanly
- "What builds trust here" section present — audits that only find faults foment their own kind of distrust

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract

- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	
95	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
96	
97	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
98	
99	## Suite-level fail-fast
100	
101	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
102	
103	## No-teardown decision
104	
105	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
106	
107	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
108	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
109	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
110	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
111	
112	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
113	
114	## Operator scope
115	
116	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
117	
118	## Coupling to ₣A_
119	
120	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
121	
122	## Locked design decisions
123	
124	- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
125	- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
126	- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.
127	
128	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
129	
130	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
131	
132	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
133	
134	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
135	
136	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
137	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
138	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
139	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
140	
141	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
142	
143	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
144	
145	## References
146	
147	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
148	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
149	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
150	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
151	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
152	
153	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAH
154	
155	## Character
156	
157	Final integration pace. Wires pristine-pass as a precondition for `/rbk-prep-release` so the upstream contribution ceremony cannot proceed without a recent pristine green. Closes the loop: pristine qualification becomes load-bearing on release prep, not optional ceremony.
158	
159	## Work
160	
161	Modify `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` to add a precondition step: verify rbw-tP ran green against the current HEAD. If not, prep-release fails-fast with operator-visible message naming the marshal-zero → pristine sequence.
162	
163	Recency contract is **exact-HEAD-match** — strongest invariant, prevents stale passes. rbw-tP green tally writes an artifact linking pass to HEAD's commit SHA; `/rbk-prep-release` reads it and refuses on mismatch. Artifact is gitignored (deliberately not committed). Artifact mechanism (file location, contents) is a mount-time decision.
164	
165	Update README "Release Procedure" section to reflect that the prep-release step now enforces rbw-tP had to pass against the very HEAD that prep-release is preparing. README references the post-success cleanup ceremony documented in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI).
166	
167	## Verification
168	
169	- `/rbk-prep-release` fails fast when no pristine pass is recorded for current HEAD
170	- After successful rbw-tP, `/rbk-prep-release` proceeds normally
171	- After successful rbw-tP followed by an unrelated commit, `/rbk-prep-release` refuses (SHA mismatch)
172	- Pristine-passed artifact is gitignored
173	- README precondition documentation references the cleanup ceremony in RELEASE.md
174	
175	## Out of scope
176	
177	- Cleanup ceremony documentation in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI)
178	- Restructuring the rest of `/rbk-prep-release` (preserved as-is)
179	- Multi-pristine-run aggregation
180	- CI integration
181	- Recency policies beyond exact-HEAD-match (other policies left for future heats if needed)
182	
183	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAI
184	
185	## Character
186	
187	Maintenance pace — re-read RELEASE.md with fresh eyes after the rest of the heat lands and apply accumulated fixes the operator identified during heat work but did not address inline. Material gauntlet-design impact: vocabulary, structure, and post-success ceremony all need RELEASE.md updates beyond simple cleanup.
188	
189	Owns post-success cleanup ceremony placement in RELEASE.md; BBAAH wires the precondition and references this section from README.
190	
191	## Work
192	
193	- Re-read RELEASE.md cold
194	- Sweep gauntlet vocabulary: introduce gauntlet-suite framing where the entire test sequence is meant; "pristine" stays for tier-vocabulary and the §1 fixture name
195	- Update §3 Sequence to reflect the gauntlet suite shape (suite-of-fixtures composed in order, not custom orchestrator-script)
196	- Update Cost framing if drift accumulated
197	- No-teardown semantics: explicitly document that rbw-tP leaves canonical infrastructure in place after green tally for operator inspection
198	- Document the post-success cleanup ceremony (`rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU`) in RELEASE.md for when operators are ready to release resources
199	- Apply other corrections, clarifications, and restructurings captured during the heat but deferred from those paces — sourced from BB heat commit messages (search for RELEASE.md references) and BBAAc walkthrough findings
200	- Verify references to rbw-tP, fixture names, and the marshal-zero signature shape match what the heat actually delivered
201	- Confirm cross-doc links from RELEASE.md to README.md anchors still resolve
202	- Confirm the README's pointer paragraph and reference-tree entry still match RELEASE.md content
203	
204	## Verification
205	
206	- Updates committed
207	- Operator confirms the doc reads correctly cold from a release-day perspective
208	- Gauntlet vocabulary, no-teardown semantics, and post-success cleanup ceremony all present and clear in RELEASE.md
209	- README anchor for cleanup ceremony resolves correctly from BBAAH's reference
210	
211	## Out of scope

463	## Scope

465	### On-curia orchestrating bash (rbf* family)

They receive parameters via `_RBG*` substitutions that the director renders into jq-composed build step bodies. They are a distinct trust surface from the orchestrating bash: quoting hazards land differently (step scripts run in a minimal container); error handling in-builder affects what exit codes the builder surfaces; step scripts see only what the director sends.

482	| `Tools/rbk/rbgja/` | 4 | About assembly — discover-platforms.py, syft-per-platform.sh, build-info-per-platform.py, assemble-push-about.sh |
